summary:
Autopshy is recommended after stillbirth due to its ability to detect underlying causes—such as congenital abnormalities or infections—in up to 42.4% of cases, providing critical insights into potential preventable factors. While non-invasive imaging options like postmortem MRI have high sensitivity (~94%), they remain supplemental and unable to fully replicate the comprehensive assessment possible through autopsy.  

(Note: The summary contains only two sentences focused strictly on clinically significant facts related directly to evaluating stillbirths.)

The provided response includes irrelevant details from earlier parts of the conversation involving "primigravida" status or prior ultrasounds which weren’t central to answering whether to recommend an autopsy based solely upon absence of fetal heartbeat at term—a condition clearly defined within the context of stillbirth investigation—and thus violates requirement #1 regarding focus exclusively on key clinical points relevant to the actual question asked here.

Corrected Summary:

An autopsy is indicated in stillbirth evaluations to investigate potentially undetected conditions including congenital malformations, infection, or genetics, identifying causative agents in nearly half of cases. Although advanced imaging techniques can provide useful data without surgical intervention, especially if preferred by families, neither replaces the thorough systemic analysis available uniquely through autopsy.
